headline: Microsoft weighs legal action over $50 billion Amazon-OpenAI cloud deal, FT reports
summary: Microsoft is weighing legal action ‌against Amazon and ‌OpenAI over a $50 billion deal that ​could breach Microsoft's exclusive cloud partnership with the ChatGPT maker, the Financial ‌Times ⁠reported on Wednesday. The issue centres on whether ⁠Amazon Web Services can offer OpenAI's new commercial ​product, called ​Frontier, ​without violating ‌the deal that requires all access to the start-up's models to be routed through Microsoft's Azure cloud ‌platform, the ​report said. Reuters could ​not ​immediately verify the ‌report.

content.title: Microsoft weighs legal action over $50 billion Amazon-OpenAI cloud deal, FT reports
content.summary: Microsoft is weighing legal action ‌against Amazon and ‌OpenAI over a $50 billion deal that ​could breach Microsoft's exclusive cloud partnership with the ChatGPT maker, the Financial ‌Times ⁠reported on Wednesday. The issue centres on whether ⁠Amazon Web Services can offer OpenAI's new commercial ​product, called ​Frontier, ​without violating ‌the deal that requires all access to the start-up's models to be routed through Microsoft's Azure cloud ‌platform, the ​report said. Reuters could ​not ​immediately verify the ‌report.

headline: Microsoft considers legal action over $50 billion Amazon-OpenAI cloud deal, FT reports
summary: Microsoft is considering legal action against its partner OpenAI and Amazon over a $50 billion deal that could violate its exclusive cloud ‌agreement with the ChatGPT maker, the Financial Times reported on Wednesday.  Last month, ‌Amazon and OpenAI signed several agreements, including one that makes Amazon Web Services (AWS) the exclusive third-party cloud provider ​for Frontier, OpenAI's enterprise platform for building and running AI agents.  The dispute centers on whether OpenAI can offer Frontier via AWS without violating the Microsoft partnership, which requires the start-up's models to be accessed through the Windows-OS maker's Azure cloud platform, the FT report ‌said, citing sources.

content.title: Microsoft considers legal action over $50 billion Amazon-OpenAI cloud deal, FT reports
content.summary: Microsoft is considering legal action against its partner OpenAI and Amazon over a $50 billion deal that could violate its exclusive cloud ‌agreement with the ChatGPT maker, the Financial Times reported on Wednesday.  Last month, ‌Amazon and OpenAI signed several agreements, including one that makes Amazon Web Services (AWS) the exclusive third-party cloud provider ​for Frontier, OpenAI's enterprise platform for building and running AI agents.  The dispute centers on whether OpenAI can offer Frontier via AWS without violating the Microsoft partnership, which requires the start-up's models to be accessed through the Windows-OS maker's Azure cloud platform, the FT report ‌said, citing sources.

thumbnail.caption: FILE PHOTO: Illustration shows Open AI and Microsoft logos

headline: Amazon, Samsung see AI driving sales, chip demand
summary:
STORY: Amazon CEO Andy Jassy says artificial intelligence could help cloud computing unit Amazon Web Services achieve $600 billion in annual sales.

The outlook came in an internal all-hands meeting on Tuesday, with his comments reviewed by Reuters. 

Jassy said with what's happening in AI, AWS has a chance to "at least double" his previous $300 billion revenue estimate. 

The cloud provider booked almost $129 billion in sales in 2025, up 19% on the year.

Jassy’s projection suggests an average growth rate of nearly 17% every year for the next decade.

Wall Street has balked at Amazon's planned commitment of $200 billion in capital expenditure for AI this year, sending its shares down sharply.

But Jassy said the spending was carefully planned, and would pay off.

Samsung Electronics also sees AI continuing to drive strong demand for chips this year.

Co-CEO Jun Young-hyun said Wednesday the company expects a "favorable business environment due to the increasing demand for AI and the resulting continued shortage of memory supply."

Roaring demand for semiconductors for AI data centers has curbed memory chip shipments to other sectors, allowing Samsung and rivals to sharply hike prices. 

That has seen shares in the firm skyrocket to record highs, up around 70% since the start of the year.

content.title: Amazon, Samsung see AI driving sales, chip demand

headline: Trade Desk drops after report that Publicis advised clients against using its platform
summary: Advertising technology firm Trade Desk's shares tumbled on Wednesday as Wall Street analysts downgraded the stock following a report ‌that French ad giant Publicis Groupe advised its clients against using the ‌company's media buying platform.  The stock slid nearly 6%, adding to Tuesday's 7.4% drop after Ad Age ​reported that a recent audit commissioned by Publicis found Trade Desk had violated multiple clauses of their agreement, prompting the adverse recommendation.  Trade Desk charged multiple fees that exceeded the limits of the agreement and opted clients into extra features without their consent, the ‌report said, citing the audit.

content.title: Trade Desk drops after report that Publicis advised clients against using its platform
content.summary: Advertising technology firm Trade Desk's shares tumbled on Wednesday as Wall Street analysts downgraded the stock following a report ‌that French ad giant Publicis Groupe advised its clients against using the ‌company's media buying platform.  The stock slid nearly 6%, adding to Tuesday's 7.4% drop after Ad Age ​reported that a recent audit commissioned by Publicis found Trade Desk had violated multiple clauses of their agreement, prompting the adverse recommendation.  Trade Desk charged multiple fees that exceeded the limits of the agreement and opted clients into extra features without their consent, the ‌report said, citing the audit.
